content.text: A transformer encoder-only model takes a sequence of tokens and produces a contextualized vector for each token position. It has
— it cannot generate text by predicting the next token. What it can do very well is
text: classify it, extract information from it, or embed it into a semantic vector space.

: every token attends to every other token, both left and right, in a single forward pass. This gives each output vector full sentence context — the representation of "bank" in "banking rules" incorporates the word "rules" just as much as the word "banking".

attrs.alt: Left: attention pattern comparison — encoder sees all tokens (full green grid), decoder-only sees only past tokens (lower triangle). Center: MLM pre-training masking 'cat' and predicting it from context. Right: BERT-base architecture with 12 stacked encoder blocks, task heads (classification, NER, QA), and variant comparison table.

content.text: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, Devlin et al., 2018) is the canonical encoder-only model. BERT-base stacks 12 identical encoder blocks. Each block has two sub-layers, each wrapped in a residual connection and LayerNorm:
: 12 attention heads, each computing scaled dot-product attention over all token pairs. Output dimension: 768.
: two linear layers with GELU activation. Inner dimension: 3072; output dimension: 768.
Input to the first block is the sum of three learned embedding tables:
(A or B, for sentence-pair inputs), and
15% of input tokens are randomly selected. Of those:
10% are replaced with a random token
The model predicts the original token at each masked position using the surrounding context from both directions. The 10%/10% split is needed because the model never sees
tokens at inference time; the noise prevents it from simply learning to copy unmasked tokens.
BERT is also trained to predict whether sentence B follows sentence A (50% real pairs, 50% random). The
token's final vector is used for this binary prediction. Later work (RoBERTa) found NSP adds little value and removed it — the MLM objective alone produces strong representations.
The pretrained BERT checkpoint is loaded and a small task-specific head is added on top. The whole network is then fine-tuned end-to-end on labeled data for a few epochs.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    "bert-base-uncased",
    num_labels=2,  # e.g. positive / negative
)

batch = tokenizer(
    ["The movie was great!", "Terrible experience."],
    padding=True,
    truncation=True,
    return_tensors="pt",
)

outputs = model(**batch)
logits = outputs.logits          # shape: (2, 2)
predictions = logits.argmax(-1)  # tensor([1, 0])
The library provides task-specific wrappers for the common patterns:

from transformers import pipeline

# Pre-fine-tuned NER model from the Hub
ner = pipeline("ner", model="dbmdz/bert-large-cased-finetuned-conll03-english",
               aggregation_strategy="simple")

result = ner("Hugging Face was founded in New York City.")
for entity in result:
    print(f"{entity['entity_group']:5s}  {entity['word']:20s}  score={entity['score']:.3f}")
# ORG   Hugging Face          score=0.998
# LOC   New York City         score=0.999
BERT uses WordPiece tokenization with a 30 522-token vocabulary. Rare or unknown words are split into subword pieces. The tokenizer always prepends

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

encoding = tokenizer("Banking rules changed.", return_tensors="pt")
print(tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(encoding["input_ids"][0].tolist()))
# ['[CLS]', 'banking', 'rules', 'changed', '.', '[SEP]']

# Subword splitting for an OOV word
print(tokenizer.tokenize("unrecognizable"))
# ['un', '##rec', '##og', '##ni', '##za', '##ble']

BERT has a 512-token limit. For longer documents, common strategies are: truncate to the first 512 tokens, use a sliding window over the document, or switch to a model with extended context (LongFormer, BigBird).

Before BERT (2018), the dominant NLP paradigm was feature extraction: take a pre-trained word embedding (Word2Vec, GloVe), feed it into a task-specific architecture, and train that architecture from scratch. Transfer learning was limited — the pre-trained representations were static and context-free. BERT changed this by showing that a single bidirectional transformer encoder, pre-trained on large text corpora, could be fine-tuned on a diverse range of downstream tasks and achieve state-of-the-art results with minimal task-specific architecture changes.

The practical impact was immediate. BERT and its derivatives (RoBERTa, DistilBERT, ALBERT) became the default starting point for classification, NER, question answering, and semantic similarity — tasks that collectively cover the vast majority of production NLP workloads. Understanding encoder-only models means understanding the architecture that powers most production NLP systems deployed today, from spam filters to search ranking.
Every token vector incorporates information from both left and right context simultaneously, producing richer representations than left-to-right language models.
A single pre-trained checkpoint transfers effectively to classification, NER, QA, and semantic similarity with only a lightweight task head added on top.
Producing an output requires a single forward pass, not a step-by-step generation loop. This makes inference fast and parallelisable.
The standard recipe — 2-4 epochs, lr=2e-5 to 5e-5, warmup then linear decay — works reliably across tasks, lowering the barrier to production deployment.
The [CLS] token vector is a high-quality fixed-length sentence representation useful for clustering, semantic search, and zero-shot classification.
BERT-base (110M parameters) is orders of magnitude smaller than GPT-3 (175B) while outperforming it on understanding tasks.
Encoder-only models have no decoder and cannot produce novel text. For summarisation, translation, or open-ended generation, a decoder or encoder-decoder model is required.
BERT has a maximum sequence length of 512 tokens. Documents longer than this must be chunked, losing cross-chunk context.
Pre-training a full BERT-base model requires ~4 days on 4 Cloud TPUs. Fine-tuning from a public checkpoint sidesteps this, but domain-specific pre-training remains expensive.
Quadratic attention cost makes BERT slow on long sequences. Efficient variants (Longformer, BigBird) address this with sparse attention patterns.
Aggressive fine-tuning at high learning rates can destroy the pre-trained representations. Low learning rates and short fine-tuning schedules mitigate this.

Use encoder-only models whenever the task requires understanding rather than generation: sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, natural language inference, semantic similarity, extractive question answering (where the answer is a span of the input), and text classification of any kind. These tasks share a common property: the full input is available before any output is produced, which means bidirectional attention is strictly better than causal (left-to-right) attention.

Do NOT use encoder-only models for: open-ended text generation, machine translation, abstractive summarisation, dialogue systems, or any task where the output must be generated token by token. For those tasks, use a decoder-only model (GPT family) or an encoder-decoder model (T5, BART). Also avoid encoder-only models when the input regularly exceeds 512 tokens — use a long-range variant or a hierarchical chunking approach.

attrs.alt: BERT fine-tuning pipeline: pre-trained checkpoint, task-specific head, fine-tuning hyperparameters, and GLUE benchmark results

content.text: The following walkthrough covers the complete fine-tuning workflow for text classification using the Hugging Face transformers library, which is the production standard.

# pip install transformers datasets torch accelerate
from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import (
    AutoTokenizer,
    AutoModelForSequenceClassification,
    TrainingArguments,
    Trainer,
    DataCollatorWithPadding,
)
import numpy as np
import evaluate

# ── 1. Load tokenizer and model ───────────────────────────────────────────────
MODEL_NAME = "bert-base-uncased"
tokenizer  = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_NAME)
model      = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    MODEL_NAME, num_labels=2   # binary sentiment classification
)

# ── 2. Load and tokenise dataset ──────────────────────────────────────────────
raw_dataset = load_dataset("imdb")

def tokenise(batch):
    return tokenizer(
        batch["text"],
        truncation=True,        # truncate to 512 tokens
        max_length=512,
    )

tokenised = raw_dataset.map(tokenise, batched=True, remove_columns=["text"])
tokenised = tokenised.rename_column("label", "labels")

# ── 3. Define evaluation metric ───────────────────────────────────────────────
accuracy_metric = evaluate.load("accuracy")

def compute_metrics(eval_pred):
    logits, labels = eval_pred
    preds = np.argmax(logits, axis=-1)
    return accuracy_metric.compute(predictions=preds, references=labels)

# ── 4. Configure training ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./bert-imdb",
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=16,
    per_device_eval_batch_size=32,
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    warmup_ratio=0.1,           # 10% warmup then linear decay
    weight_decay=0.01,
    evaluation_strategy="epoch",
    save_strategy="epoch",
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
    metric_for_best_model="accuracy",
    fp16=True,                  # half-precision: 2× memory reduction on GPU
    logging_steps=100,
)

# ── 5. Train ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=tokenised["train"],
    eval_dataset=tokenised["test"],
    tokenizer=tokenizer,
    data_collator=DataCollatorWithPadding(tokenizer),
    compute_metrics=compute_metrics,
)

trainer.train()
# Typical output: eval_accuracy = 0.937 after 3 epochs

# ── 6. Save and reload ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
trainer.save_model("./bert-imdb-final")
# Later:
# model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained("./bert-imdb-final")
Real-World Example: Sentence Embeddings for Semantic Search

A common production use of encoder-only models is semantic search: encode a query and a set of candidate documents into dense vectors, then retrieve the nearest neighbours by cosine similarity. This is faster and more accurate than keyword matching for paraphrased queries.

# pip install sentence-transformers faiss-cpu
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np
import faiss

# ── sentence-transformers wraps BERT/RoBERTa for efficient embedding ──────────
model = SentenceTransformer("all-mpnet-base-v2")  # encoder-only, 420M params

DOCUMENTS = [
    "How to file income tax returns in the United States",
    "Best practices for securing Kubernetes clusters",
    "Introduction to quantum computing algorithms",
    "Python type hints and static analysis with mypy",
    "Understanding transformer attention mechanisms",
    "Guide to BERT fine-tuning for text classification",
    "Tax deductions available for remote workers",
    "Zero-trust network architecture design patterns",
]

# ── Encode documents once and store in a FAISS index ─────────────────────────
doc_embeddings = model.encode(DOCUMENTS, normalize_embeddings=True)  # (N, 768)
d = doc_embeddings.shape[1]
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(d)   # Inner product ≡ cosine sim for unit vectors
index.add(doc_embeddings.astype(np.float32))

def semantic_search(query: str, top_k: int = 3) -> list[tuple[str, float]]:
    q_emb = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
    scores, indices = index.search(q_emb, top_k)
    return [(DOCUMENTS[i], float(scores[0][j])) for j, i in enumerate(indices[0])]

print(semantic_search("How do I optimise BERT for NLP tasks?"))
# [('Guide to BERT fine-tuning for text classification', 0.72),
#  ('Understanding transformer attention mechanisms', 0.61),
#  ('Python type hints and static analysis with mypy', 0.42)]

print(semantic_search("tax filing for people working from home"))
# [('Tax deductions available for remote workers', 0.81),
#  ('How to file income tax returns in the United States', 0.74),
#  ('Best practices for securing Kubernetes clusters', 0.18)]
Using [CLS] from raw BERT for sentence similarity

# WRONG: raw BERT's [CLS] token is trained for MLM classification, NOT
# sentence-level similarity. Cosine similarities cluster near zero and
# do not correlate with semantic meaning.
from transformers import AutoModel, AutoTokenizer
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model     = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

def bad_similarity(s1, s2):
    enc1 = tokenizer(s1, return_tensors="pt")
    enc2 = tokenizer(s2, return_tensors="pt")
    with torch.no_grad():
        v1 = model(**enc1).last_hidden_state[:, 0, :]  # [CLS]
        v2 = model(**enc2).last_hidden_state[:, 0, :]
    cos = torch.nn.functional.cosine_similarity(v1, v2)
    return cos.item()

# bad_similarity("cats are pets", "dogs are pets")  →  ~0.98 (wrong: too high for everything)
# bad_similarity("cats are pets", "stock market crash")  →  ~0.96

# RIGHT: Use sentence-transformers which applies mean pooling + contrastive training
# from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
# model = SentenceTransformer("all-mpnet-base-v2")
Setting learning rate too high during fine-tuning

# WRONG: large learning rate causes catastrophic forgetting
training_args_bad = TrainingArguments(
    learning_rate=1e-3,   # 100× too high; destroys pre-trained representations
    num_train_epochs=10,
)

# RIGHT: keep lr in 1e-5 to 5e-5 range for fine-tuning
training_args_good = TrainingArguments(
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    warmup_ratio=0.1,     # warmup prevents large updates in early steps
    num_train_epochs=3,   # more epochs rarely helps; overfitting risk increases
    weight_decay=0.01,    # L2 regularisation on non-bias parameters
)

# WRONG: BERT errors or silently truncates inputs > 512 tokens without truncation=True
enc = tokenizer("very long document ..." * 100)   # may raise or silently truncate

# RIGHT: always pass truncation=True and max_length=512
enc = tokenizer(
    "very long document ...",
    truncation=True,
    max_length=512,
    return_tensors="pt"
)
Use BioBERT for medical text, Legal-BERT for legal text, FinBERT for financial text. Domain-specific pre-training gives a significant accuracy boost over BERT-base.
Use DistilBERT or ALBERT for production inference.
DistilBERT is 40% smaller, 60% faster, and retains 97% of BERT's accuracy on GLUE. Use the full BERT only if the accuracy gap matters for your task.
Freeze early layers when data is scarce.
With fewer than 1000 labelled examples, freeze BERT's first 8-10 layers and fine-tune only the top 2-4 layers plus the task head. This prevents overfitting.
Always use a linear warmup scheduler.
Start with 5-10% of total steps as warmup. Without warmup, the first high-gradient steps can permanently damage the pre-trained representations.
Evaluate with macro-F1 on imbalanced datasets.
Accuracy is misleading when one class dominates. Use macro-averaged F1 or per-class precision/recall as the primary metric.
The sentence-transformers library provides fine-tuned BERT variants with mean-pooled embeddings that are directly comparable via cosine similarity.

BERT-base has 110M parameters and requires ~440MB of GPU memory for inference with a batch size of 1. BERT-large (340M parameters) requires ~1.3GB. For batch inference, memory scales linearly with batch size. A batch of 32 sequences of length 128 on a single A100 GPU processes at approximately 1000 sequences per second.

attrs.latex: \text{Attention memory per layer} = O(T^2 \cdot d_h) \text{ where } T = \text{seq length}, d_h = \text{head dim}

content.text: The quadratic memory cost of attention limits sequence length. BERT is practical for T ≤ 512. For longer documents, use: (1) chunking with stride (process overlapping 512-token windows, merge predictions); (2) hierarchical models (encode sentences with BERT, aggregate with a lightweight model); or (3) efficient attention models (Longformer, BigBird) with linear or logarithmic attention cost.

# Export BERT to ONNX for production inference
# pip install optimum
from optimum.onnxruntime import ORTModelForSequenceClassification
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

# Export a fine-tuned model to ONNX
ort_model = ORTModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    "./bert-imdb-final", export=True
)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")

# Inference: ~3-4× faster than PyTorch on CPU
inputs = tokenizer("This movie was fantastic!", return_tensors="pt")
outputs = ort_model(**inputs)
import torch
pred = torch.argmax(outputs.logits, dim=-1).item()
print("Positive" if pred == 1 else "Negative")

Fine-tuned BERT models trained on private data may memorise sensitive information from training examples. Named entity recognition models trained on medical records can leak patient names if queried with adversarial inputs designed to trigger memorised associations. Standard mitigations: differential privacy during fine-tuning (using the DP-SGD algorithm via the Opacus library), regular audits of model outputs for PII, and access controls on model endpoints.

Reliability risks: (1) BERT is sensitive to tokenisation quirks — unusual Unicode characters, emojis, and non-English text can produce unexpected tokenisations that degrade accuracy; (2) fine-tuned models trained on English data degrade significantly on multilingual input even when the primary model is BERT-multilingual; (3) distribution shift between training data and production data is the most common cause of accuracy degradation — monitor prediction confidence distributions in production.
Enforce maximum sequence length at the API boundary; return an error rather than silently truncating long inputs.
Return 'uncertain' when max softmax probability is below 0.7; flag for human review.
Pin the tokeniser and model versions together; a tokeniser update can break a fine-tuned model.
Track distribution of predicted class probabilities in production. A shift in average confidence is an early signal of distribution shift.
Encoder-only models produce bidirectional contextualised representations by attending to all tokens simultaneously — unlike causal models that can only attend leftward.
BERT's two pre-training objectives — MLM (predict masked tokens) and NSP (predict sentence order) — were jointly trained on BooksCorpus + Wikipedia (3.3B words).
Fine-tuning adds a lightweight task-specific head (typically one linear layer) on top of the frozen or partially-frozen BERT encoder; 2-4 epochs at lr=2e-5 is the standard recipe.
The [CLS] token vector is a global sentence representation used for classification; token vectors are used for token-level tasks like NER and span extraction.
Encoder-only models cannot generate text — they require a decoder or encoder-decoder architecture for generative tasks.
DistilBERT and RoBERTa are the practical first choices: DistilBERT for speed, RoBERTa for accuracy.
For semantic similarity and search, use sentence-transformers rather than raw BERT [CLS] vectors, which are not calibrated for cosine similarity.
The maximum sequence length of 512 tokens is a hard constraint; use Longformer, BigBird, or hierarchical chunking for long-document tasks.
